ideas.title: The Maternal Mortality Scandal
ideas.hook: The US maternal mortality rate (23.8 per 100,000) is nearly 8x higher than Norway's (3.0) — the largest gap between any two G7 countries on a basic health outcome.
ideas.peer_angle: Rich-vs-poor comparison makes this look like a development problem. Peer comparison reveals it's a policy problem: every other G7 nation solved it decades ago.
ideas.research_question: Why does the wealthiest country in the world have a maternal death rate comparable to middle-income nations, while its peers do not?
ideas.data_source: WHO Global Health Observatory; CDC MMWR
ideas.reason: Evergreen political flashpoint with hard numbers most people don't know; drives strong emotional engagement across audiences

ideas.title: Less School, Better Scores
ideas.hook: Finnish students spend 593 hours per year in school — the fewest in the OECD — yet consistently outscore Americans, who spend 1,080 hours.
ideas.peer_angle: Among wealthy OECD nations, the correlation between instructional hours and PISA performance is actually slightly negative.
ideas.research_question: What does time spent in school actually predict, if not academic achievement?
ideas.data_source: OECD Education at a Glance; PISA 2022 Results
ideas.reason: Challenges a universal intuition about effort and output; highly shareable among parents and educators

ideas.title: The Antidepressant Paradox
ideas.hook: Iceland consumes more antidepressants per capita (106 defined daily doses per 1,000 people) than any OECD nation, yet ranks 3rd globally in happiness.
ideas.peer_angle: Nordic peer nations have similarly high usage and similarly high happiness, collapsing the stigma-driven assumption that medication use signals societal failure.
ideas.research_question: Does antidepressant consumption reflect dysfunction, or functioning mental healthcare access?
ideas.data_source: OECD Health Statistics (Pharmaceutical Consumption); World Happiness Report
ideas.reason: Directly challenges mental health stigma with data; counterintuitive enough to generate genuine debate

ideas.title: Free Press, Mistrusted Press
ideas.hook: The US ranks 55th in press freedom but only 32% of Americans trust the news — while Finland ranks 1st in press freedom and 69% of Finns trust it.
ideas.peer_angle: Among peer democracies, high press freedom does not guarantee public trust — the gap between the UK and Nordic countries shows this within Western Europe.
ideas.research_question: What determines whether a free press is also a trusted press?
ideas.data_source: RSF World Press Freedom Index; Reuters Institute Digital News Report 2024
ideas.reason: Timely amid ongoing media credibility crisis; interactive map lets users explore their own country's divergence

ideas.title: The Carbon Tax Experiment
ideas.hook: Sweden introduced a carbon tax in 1991 at $30/ton (now $130/ton); since then, emissions fell 27% while GDP grew 75%.
ideas.peer_angle: Neighboring Norway and Denmark adopted similar mechanisms on different timelines — comparing peer Nordics isolates the policy effect from cultural confounds.
ideas.research_question: Is carbon pricing the most reliable decoupling mechanism among wealthy nations, or is it confounded by other factors?
ideas.data_source: Swedish Environmental Protection Agency; World Bank Carbon Pricing Dashboard; OECD National Accounts
ideas.reason: Directly addresses the most common objection to climate policy ('it hurts the economy') with 30 years of real data

ideas.title: How You Vote Determines Who Votes
ideas.hook: Countries using proportional representation average 35% women in parliament; majoritarian systems average 22% — a gap that persists even after controlling for region and cultural attitudes.
ideas.peer_angle: Among Western peer democracies, the UK (first-past-the-post, 35%) and New Zealand (switched to PR in 1996, now 50%) offer a near-natural experiment.
ideas.research_question: How much of the gender gap in legislatures is an artifact of the voting system itself?
ideas.data_source: IPU Parline Global Data on National Parliaments; IDEA Electoral System Design database
ideas.reason: Evergreen democratic reform topic; the New Zealand before/after is a compelling narrative hook

ideas.title: Recycling's Dirty Secret
ideas.hook: Germany recycles 66% of its municipal waste — highest in the OECD — yet sends more plastic to Southeast Asian countries for processing than Sweden, which recycles far less but incinerates nearly everything domestically.
ideas.peer_angle: Within European peers, the gap between recycling rate and actual landfill/leakage outcomes is revealing — high-recycling countries often externalize the problem.
ideas.research_question: Is recycling rate a meaningful environmental metric, or a displacement activity that makes wealthy nations feel virtuous?
ideas.data_source: Eurostat Municipal Waste Statistics; OECD Global Plastic Outlook
ideas.reason: Challenges one of the most widely held environmental beliefs with uncomfortable data

ideas.title: The Misinformation Immunity Map
ideas.hook: Finland, which introduced national media literacy curriculum in 2014, has the lowest misinformation susceptibility score in Europe (77/100); Italy, with no equivalent program, scores 37/100.
ideas.peer_angle: Within the EU, countries that joined at similar economic development levels diverge sharply based on media education policy — not wealth or internet access.
ideas.research_question: Can media literacy education be measured as a policy intervention with cross-country evidence?
ideas.data_source: Open Society Foundations Media Literacy Index; Reuters Institute; European Digital Media Observatory
ideas.reason: Directly actionable policy question in an era of information crisis; Finland's curriculum is a concrete case study

ideas.title: More Doctors, Worse Health
ideas.hook: Greece has 6.1 physicians per 1,000 people — the highest ratio in the EU — yet ranks below the Netherlands (3.6/1,000) on virtually every major health outcome measure.
ideas.peer_angle: Within the EU peer group, physician density has near-zero correlation with life expectancy or preventable mortality rates.
ideas.research_question: If doctor supply doesn't predict health outcomes, what does?
ideas.data_source: OECD Health at a Glance 2023; Eurostat Preventable Mortality Statistics
ideas.reason: Challenges the intuitive fix ('just train more doctors') with cross-country evidence

ideas.title: The Sleep Debt of Nations
ideas.hook: Japanese workers average 442 minutes of sleep per night — the least in the OECD — while Dutch workers average 518 minutes and produce 40% more economic output per hour.
ideas.peer_angle: The sleep-productivity gap is largest within wealthy Asian vs. European peer comparison; extreme global comparison would confound with poverty.
ideas.research_question: Is sleep deprivation a hidden drag on national productivity, or does causation run the other way?
ideas.data_source: OECD Time Use Database; OECD Compendium of Productivity Indicators
ideas.reason: Universally relatable; connects personal behavior to national-scale data in a surprising way

ideas.title: Democracy's Satisfaction Gap
ideas.hook: Switzerland has among the lowest voter turnout in Western Europe (45%) yet the highest citizen satisfaction with democracy — because it holds 4–5 national referendums per year.
ideas.peer_angle: Among wealthy European democracies, turnout and democratic satisfaction are weakly correlated; the mechanism matters more than the act.
ideas.research_question: Does voter turnout measure democratic health, or just mandatory participation in a system people feel powerless within?
ideas.data_source: IDEA Voter Turnout Database; Eurobarometer Survey on Democracy; Swiss Federal Chancellery
ideas.reason: Reframes a core assumption about what 'healthy democracy' looks like; timely amid declining turnout debates

ideas.title: The Degree Inflation Trap
ideas.hook: Canada has the highest share of adults with university degrees in the OECD (67%) but files fewer patents per researcher than Israel (55% degree attainment) or Switzerland (47%).
ideas.peer_angle: Among wealthy OECD peers, degree saturation above ~50% shows diminishing returns on innovation output — the credential has decoupled from the skill.
ideas.research_question: At what point does university expansion stop producing innovation and start producing credential inflation?
ideas.data_source: OECD Education at a Glance 2023; WIPO Patent Statistics
ideas.reason: Challenges the bipartisan consensus that 'more degrees = better economy' with cross-country evidence

ideas.title: The Judicial Independence Dividend
ideas.hook: Countries where judges are appointed through merit-based processes have a corruption perceptions score 22 points higher on average than countries where judges face popular elections.
ideas.peer_angle: The US is the primary wealthy outlier using judicial elections for some courts — comparing it to peer common-law democracies (UK, Canada, Australia) isolates the effect.
ideas.research_question: Does democratic accountability in the judiciary improve or undermine the rule of law?
ideas.data_source: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index; Transparency International CPI; Comparative Constitutions Project
ideas.reason: Timely given ongoing judicial reform debates globally; uses data to reframe a values debate

ideas.title: Clean Air, Sick Lungs
ideas.hook: Australia has some of the cleanest outdoor air in the OECD yet has the highest asthma prevalence rate (21%) — nearly double Germany's, which has worse air quality metrics.
ideas.peer_angle: Among wealthy peer nations, outdoor air quality has weak predictive power over asthma rates — indoor air, allergen exposure, and hygiene hypothesis effects dominate.
ideas.research_question: What actually drives asthma rates in wealthy nations, if outdoor pollution is not the primary culprit?
ideas.data_source: WHO Ambient Air Quality Database; Global Burden of Disease Study (IHME); AIHW
ideas.reason: Counterintuitive enough to stop scrolling; opens a richer conversation about environmental health causation

ideas.title: Where Alcohol Kills Differently
ideas.hook: France and Finland consume similar amounts of alcohol per capita (~12L pure alcohol/year), but Finland's liver disease mortality rate is nearly 3x higher — because Finns drink to get drunk while French drink with meals.
ideas.peer_angle: Among European peer nations with similar consumption levels, pattern of drinking (binge vs. distributed) predicts liver mortality better than total volume consumed.
ideas.research_question: Does how a nation drinks matter more than how much it drinks?
ideas.data_source: WHO Global Status Report on Alcohol and Health 2022; Eurostat Causes of Death Statistics
ideas.reason: Challenges the simple 'drink less' message with a nuanced behavioral finding; culturally fascinating
